text: The energy conversion process in photosynthesis starts with the light-driven cleavage of water that generates electrons, protons, and molecular oxygen. This process is catalyzed by the $\text{Mn}_4\text{CaO}_5$ cluster in photosystem II. In order to facilitate the O–O bond formation, the cluster accumulates four oxidizing equivalents corresponding to the $S_0$ through $S_4$ states of the Kok cycle, which is driven by photochemical charge separation processes occurring in the reaction center. Here, we apply room-temperature serial femtosecond X-ray crystallography to probe the $S_3 \rightarrow [S_4] \rightarrow S_0$ process occurring during the oxygen evolution and the subsequent resetting of the catalytic clock. These measurements reveal a complex dynamics occurring over the time frame of several micro- to millisecond(s), comprising $\text{Mn}_4\text{CaO}_5$ cluster and ligand structural rearrangements, changes in water access channels, and concerted proton release through the Cl1 hydrogen bond channel. Furthermore, the additional oxygen atom ($O_x$) that bridges the Ca and Mn1 upon the $S_2 \rightarrow S_3$ transition either disappears or relocates around 700 µs after the third flash, consistent with the $Y_Z$ reduction. The onset of oxygen evolution is accompanied by a shortening of the Mn1–Mn4 distance at approximately 1,200 µs, indicating the formation of a reduced intermediate possibly corresponding to a bound peroxide prior to the $S_0$ state restoration.
